coredata.dc:description: While interfacial effects are critical for tuning catalytic active sites, the mechanism of optimizing molecular oxygen activation via interfacial electronic interactions under mild conditions remains underexplored. This study reports a carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/calcined CuCoFe-LDH (CuCoFe-300) composite, where C=O groups on CQDs form interfacial hydrogen-bond electron channels (C=O⋯H-O) with hydroxyl groups near oxygen vacancies (OVs). These channels promote the directional transfer of electrons from CQDs to CuCoFe-300, driving electron accumulation near OVs, enhancing local charge density, with the Bader charge increasing from −0.31 (CuCoFe-300) to −0.48 e (CQDs/CuCoFe-300). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicate that the O2 adsorption energy increases from −0.27 to −0.34 eV, and the O-O bond length stretches from 1.209 to 1.29 Å, while the original CuCoFe-300 is only 1.27 Å, which facilitates O-O bond cleavage. The synergistic effect of the interfacial hydrogen bond network and OVs achieves the conversion of triplet O2 to singlet oxygen (1O2) by injecting electrons into the O2 antibonding orbitals. Compared to CuCoFe-300, the electronic transfer rate in the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalytic system is enhanced by 5.87 times, and the yield of 1O2 increases by 3.42 times, thus achieving a 90 % degradation efficiency of tetracycline under mild and high salinity conditions. This study proposes a new strategy to enhance the activation of molecular oxygen by adjusting interfacial electronic interactions, providing insights for the design of efficient and low-energy catalytic systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synergistic effect between the interface hydrogen bond electronic channels and oxygen vacancies enhances the activation efficiency of O2.

While interfacial effects are critical for tuning catalytic active sites, the mechanism of optimizing molecular oxygen activation via interfacial electronic interactions under mild conditions remains underexplored. This study reports a carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/calcined CuCoFe-LDH (CuCoFe-300) composite, where C=O groups on CQDs form interfacial hydrogen-bond electron channels (C=O⋯H-O) with hydroxyl groups near oxygen vacancies (OVs). These channels promote the directional transfer of electrons from CQDs to CuCoFe-300, driving electron accumulation near OVs, enhancing local charge density, with the Bader charge increasing from −0.31 (CuCoFe-300) to −0.48 e (CQDs/CuCoFe-300). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicate that the O2 adsorption energy increases from −0.27 to −0.34 eV, and the O-O bond length stretches from 1.209 to 1.29 Å, while the original CuCoFe-300 is only 1.27 Å, which facilitates O-O bond cleavage. The synergistic effect of the interfacial hydrogen bond network and OVs achieves the conversion of triplet O2 to singlet oxygen (1O2) by injecting electrons into the O2 antibonding orbitals. Compared to CuCoFe-300, the electronic transfer rate in the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalytic system is enhanced by 5.87 times, and the yield of 1O2 increases by 3.42 times, thus achieving a 90 % degradation efficiency of tetracycline under mild and high salinity conditions. This study proposes a new strategy to enhance the activation of molecular oxygen by adjusting interfacial electronic interactions, providing insights for the design of efficient and low-energy catalytic systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The massive discharge of antibiotic wastewater from the pharmaceutical industry into the environment poses significant threats to human health and ecological systems, with tailwater concentrations reaching as high as mg/L [1]. This makes antibiotic wastewater a persistent emerging pollutant of great concern in the environmental field in recent years [2]. Tetracycline (TC), one of the most commonly used antibiotics, is frequently detected in receiving water. Due to the limitations of conventional wastewater treatment processes, the ecological toxicity and bioaccumulation of water environments have significantly increased [3]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop efficient control strategies for TC pollution in water. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have attracted considerable attention for TC degradation due to their advantages of strong oxidation ability and rapid reaction rates [4,5]. AOPs generate abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which can enhance the degradation efficiency of organic pollutants. However, AOPs usually require the addition of strong oxidants or external energy inputs, which greatly increase the energy consumption and treatment costs of the reaction system. In this context, the catalyst physicochemical properties have a significant effect on the treatment performance of AOPs technology [6]. Hence, the development of highly active catalysts to mitigate system energy consumption is expected to promote the sustainable development of AOPs in the antibiotic wastewater treatment field.

In recent years, increasing the electron transfer rate at the catalyst interface is recognized as an effective strategy to enhance the catalyst performance [7]. Benefiting from electronic interactions at the interface between the catalyst and its surrounding medium, various anomalous redox processes are triggered due to the specific chemical and physical properties of the interface effect [8]. Previous research on the construction of interfacial electronic channels indicated that mass and electron transfer behavior at the interface was easier and faster, contributing to improved electron transport rates and electron utilization during reaction processes [9,10]. Notably, the electron transfer rate was closely related to the ROS generation. It was reported that the interfacial electronic channels were constructed in mesoporous structure via etching Bi on the surface of metal–organic frameworks, which facilitated electron transport rate for the rapid generation of •O2 –, thus accelerating the degradation of organic pollutants [11]. A bimetallic sulfides/N-doped reduced graphene oxide composite with an interfacial electronic channel structure was found to activate peroxymonosulfate more effectively due to its rapid mass and electron transfer capability, and •O2 – and 1O2 were verified as major ROS responsible for sulfamethoxazole degradation [12]. By doping Ca into g-C3N4, a transmission channel for electron migration were constructed, altering the local electronic state and promoting the formation of abundant ROS [13]. These findings demonstrated that constructing interfacial electronic channels on catalysts effectively enhanced electron transfer efficiency and accelerated ROS generation, which was crucial for improving the pollutant degradation performance. Therefore, materials with the specific surface properties should be considered as optimal candidates.

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are non-noble metal materials with abundant functional groups and surface defect structures at the nanoscale [14], making them ideal materials for constructing interfacial electronic channels. For instance, a distinctive interfacial electronic channel was engineered by loading α-Ga2O3 on the LDHs surface, significantly accelerating the formation rate of 1O2 and promoting the mineralization of volatile organic compounds [15]. Another example is the formation of an interfacial electronic channel through phosphotungstic acid-modified ZnAl-LDH, which accelerated the electron transport and mass transfer processes to form oxygen radicals, improving the catalytic reaction rate [16]. Among various surface modifiers, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were noted for being inexpensive, environmentally friendly, and low in toxicity [17]. Particularly, CQDs were negatively charged and possessed abundant surface functional groups, making them highly compatible with LDHs materials and easily adjustable to meet the requirements for preparing an interfacial channel on the LDHs surface. According to reports, interfacial electronic channels were formed via CQDs loaded on MnCo-LDH, which significantly increased the density of states near the Fermi level, thereby enhancing the transfer rate of interfacial electrons [18]. Inspired by these findings, loading CQDs on LDHs may be a superior strategy for constructing interfacial electronic channels. Currently, there are no reported studies on the construction of CQDs on LDHs with oxygen vacancies (OVs) to form interfacial electron transfer channels, enhance the electron utilization of OVs, and promote the formation of ROS to improve antibiotic degradation efficiency.

In this work, we developed a novel CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst for the efficient catalytic wet oxidation degradation of TC under ambient conditions. Previous work has confirmed that after calcination at 300 °C, CuCoFe-LDH undergoes a transition of its metal ions to low-energy states, resulting in oxygen deficiency and the formation of abundant OVs [19]. The surface morphologies and topological structure were evaluated to obtain the basic physicochemical properties of the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst. Molecular oxygen activation was explored under the construction of interfacial hydrogen bond electron channels for efficient pollutants degradation, and expanded the knowledge of the structure-dependent reaction mechanism. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations and electrochemical characterization were employed to evaluate the electric field properties of electronic channels, including connection modes, charge transport paths and electron transfer efficiency. A series of TC degradation behavior using CQDs/CuCoFe-300 was conducted, including catalytic kinetics, the effect of coexisting ions, and pH (4 ∼ 11) variations, to determine key parameters in the degradation process. Based on ambient conditions and air as the oxidant, the energy consumption for the treatment of TC by the reaction system combining air and the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst was found to be 0.004 kWh·L-1. This represents a significantly lower energy consumption compared to most reported advanced oxidation technologies, highlighting its broad application potential in the field of treating emerging pollutants such as antibiotics.

A mixed metal salt solution of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was prepared in a molar ratio of 1:1:1. An alkaline solution was prepared by mixing NaOH and Na2CO3 in a molar ratio of 12:1, with a NaOH/Cu(NO3)2·3H2O molar ratio of 6:1 (previous study described catalysts performance with various metal component molar ratios in detail) [19]. Using a co-precipitation method, two solutions were introduced into a glass bottle at a uniform rate, maintaining a pH range of 8.4–8.6 throughout the preparation process. The preparation was conducted at 60 °C under N2 as a protective gas. Subsequently, the suspension was aged at 60 °C for 24 h. The resulting product was washed several times with ethanol and ultra-pure water passed through N2, then dehydrated in a vacuum drying oven and ground into fine powder. Finally, the obtained solid was calcined at 300 °C in a tube furnace under N2 atmosphere for 4 h and was designated as CuCoFe-300.

CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method [20]. Specifically, 1.05 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved thoroughly in 15 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 335 μL of ethylenediamine. The resulting solution was stirred at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was then transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 453.15 K for 5 h. The CQDs solution was obtained by dialysis the resulting dark brown liquid for at least 48 h.

The CuCoFe-300 was functionalized with CQDs via electrostatic attraction method. Specifically, 1.4 g of CuCoFe-300 and 10 mL of CQDs were dispersed into 40 mL of deionized water and stirred continuously under N2 protection for 12 h. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. The resulting sediment (CQDs/CuCoFe-300) was filtered, thoroughly washed with deionized water, and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60℃.

The catalytic kinetics of TC degradation was evaluated by batch experiments conducted in glass reactors containing 200 mL of 15 mg/L TC solution. Before initiating the reaction, the solution was aerated through an aeration tube to ensure saturation of dissolved oxygen. The reaction commenced with the addition of the catalyst to the TC solution. Samples of 1 mL were extracted at 5, 10, 20, 30, 60, and 120 min after the start of the reaction and were subsequently filtered. Methanol was simultaneously added as a quenching agent to terminate the reaction.

Additional experimental details, including the chemicals, ROS quantitative measurements, mineralization rate, energy consumption calculation, DFT calculation methods, and characterization techniques were provided in Text S1, S2, S3, S4 and S5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of CQDs/CuCoFe-300
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were employed to study the morphology and microstructure of the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst. As shown in Fig. 1 b, CuCoFe-300 exhibited a sheet-like structure with good dispersibility. After liquid-phase mixing and ultrasonic treatment, CQDs were uniformly anchored on the CuCoFe-300 surface, and the edges became rougher ( Fig. S4 ). Importantly, a clear interfacial boundary was observed in CQDs/CuCoFe-300 (Fig. 1 c), suggesting a possible specific interaction between the two substances at the interface. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to better identify the materials on either side of the boundary. Fig. 1 d-e showed the enlarged lattice fringes on CQDs/CuCoFe-300 surface from Fig. 1 c, with annotations indicating that interplanar spacings of 0.279 nm and 0.232 nm corresponded to (1 1 0) and (015) crystal planes of CuCoFe-300, respectively, while the 0.208 nm spacing corresponded to the (002) plane of CQDs [23]. SEM images displayed that after loading CQDs, the microstructure of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 remained similar to that of CuCoFe-300, indicating that the nanoscale loading of CQDs did not disrupt the structure of CuCoFe-300, ruling out potential influences from morphology effects on subsequent tests (Fig. 1 f-g).

Furthermore, selected area electron diffrac (SAED) revealed the presence of the characteristic (002) plane of CQDs on the surface of CQDs/CuCoFe-300, further confirming that CQDs were successfully constructed on CuCoFe-300 ( Fig. S5 ). However, TEM and SEM images alone were insufficient to confirm whether there was a strong interaction at the interface between CQDs and CuCoFe-300. Subsequent analyses, including X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and DFT calculations, confirmed significant changes in the electronic properties of CuCoFe-300 after constructing CQDs, which were attributed to interfacial interactions rather than simple physical mixing. Correspondingly, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1 h) revealed that the characteristic crystal planes associated with LDH in CuCoFe-300 gradually disappeared, and diffraction peaks become broader due to the formation of defect structures. Notably, characteristic peaks of CuCoO4 appeared at 37.2° and 64.3° (JCPDS No. 36–1189). After the CQDs were loaded, the (002) peak of CQDs emerged on the CuCoFe-300 crystal planes, while no new diffraction peaks were observed. This indicates that the interaction between the two materials was relatively weak, and did not lead to lattice distortion or the formation of new crystalline phases.

The surface chemical state of the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst was investigated through XPS. The XPS survey scan of the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst revealed the presence of all constituent elements (Fig. 2
a). After loading CQDs onto CuCoFe-300, the content of C and O elements on the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 surface significantly increased, attributed to the introduction of numerous –OH and –CO groups from the CQDs onto the CuCoFe-300 surface. In the split and fit of C 1 s peak (Fig. 2 b), the binding energies at 284.62, 286.02 and 288.61 eV were assigned to C–C/C=C, C-O, and C=O bonds, respectively [24]. Compared with the C=O peak in CuCoFe-300, a shift of 0.18 eV toward higher binding energy in CQDs/CuCoFe-300, indicated that the C atom might lose electrons and function as an electron donor. Additionally, the O 1 s XPS spectra indicated three diffraction peaks at binding energies of 530.78, 531.54 and 532.78 eV (Fig. 2 c), corresponding to lattice oxygen, adsorbed oxygen species on OVs (e.g., O- and O2 2–), and surface hydroxyl groups, respectively [25,26]. Compared to CuCoFe-300, the characteristic peaks of OVs and surface hydroxyl groups on CQDs/CuCoFe-300 shifted to lower binding energies by 0.27 and 0.22 eV, respectively, demonstrated that O atoms and OVs might gain electrons and act as electron receptor. Notably, the shifted in the OV binding energy was greater than that of surface hydroxyl groups, implying that the electrons in CQDs tended to be transferred to the positively charged OV sites. In the Co 2p, Cu 2p and Fe 2p XPS spectra, only slight shifts (∼0.1 eV) in the binding energies of Co-O, Cu–O, and Fe-O bonds were observed ( Fig. S6 ), suggesting that electron gain and loss primarily occurred at the OV and surface hydroxyl groups rather than through interactions with the metal complexes. This was evident because CuCoFe-300, as a hexagonal crystal structure (Fig. 2 d), presented significant steric hindrance when the C=O bonds in CQDs attempted to interact with metal atoms in the bulk phase, particularly when navigating past the hydroxyl groups on the surface. Consequently, C=O bonds were considered to be more prone to interact with surface hydroxyl groups.

By optimizing the initial configuration and iterating to energy convergence, the system investigated the adsorption characteristics of CQDs on two types of active sites, hydroxyl groups and OV sites, on CuCoFe-300 (The OV structure could be observed in the top view of CQDs/CuCoFe-300, Fig. S8 ). Thermodynamic analysis (Fig. 2 d-e) showed that the adsorption energies of CQDs on both the surface hydroxyl groups and OV sites of CuCoFe-300 were negative, with values of −1.37 eV and −1.11 eV, respectively, confirming that the adsorption of CQDs on CuCoFe-300 was a spontaneous interfacial process. Notably, the adsorption energy at the hydroxyl group site is significantly higher than that at the OV site, suggesting a stronger chemisorption interaction between the CQDs molecules and the hydroxyl groups on the CuCoFe-300 surface. Further analysis of crystal stabilization energy revealed that the stable energy density of the CQDs adsorption system formed on the surface hydroxyl groups (36.17 eV/Å2) was lower than that on the OV sites (36.42 eV/Å2). This energy difference (ΔE = 0.25 eV/Å2) provided kinetic evidence that the adsorption configuration of CQDs on the surface hydroxyl groups has better structural stability. The combined analysis of thermodynamic and kinetic parameters indicated that the adsorption process of CQDs on the CuCoFe-300 exhibits a clear site preference, with a priority for stabilization on the surface hydroxyl groups rather than the OV regions.

To further understand the interfacial behavior of CQDs on CuCoFe-300, FT-IR and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques were employed to reveal their interaction forms. As shown in Fig. 2 f, the vibrational peaks at wavenumbers 500.8, 1397.9, and 1483.1 cm−1 in CuCoFe-300 were associated with M−O−M (where M denotes metal atoms) and interlayer carbonates, respectively [27]. The peaks at wavenumbers 1630.4 and 3420.1 cm−1 corresponded to bound water (H2O) and H-O bonds, respectively [28]. Notably, after loading with CQDs, a peak belonging to the C=O bond of CQDs appeared at 1637.9 cm−1 in CQDs/CuCoFe-300. Compared to CuCoFe-300, the surface H-O bonds in CQD/CuCoFe-300 exhibited a noticeable redshift. In contrast, when compared to the standalone CQDs, their electronic structure and vibrational modes became more compact due to their built on the CuCoFe-300 interface, resulting in a blueshift. Importantly, no new vibrational peaks were observed (not even weak peaks). Combined with the shift of the C=O and H-O bonds in XPS and the change in adsorption energy, these results strongly indicated that the C=O of CQDs interacted with the hydroxyl groups on the CuCoFe-300 surface in the form of hydrogen bonding (C=O…H-O), rather than forming a chemical bond (C=O–H-O). The 1H NMR spectrum of Fig. S9 showed that the 1H signals of CuCoFe-300 exhibit chemical shifts in the range of 3–8 ppm (corresponding to hydroxyl protons) after loading CQDs [29], along with a sharp decrease in signal intensity, indicating the frequent formation of multiple intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The crystal orbital Hamilton population (COHP) analysis was typically used to assess bonding forms and bond strengths [30]. By calculating the connection strength of the O…H in C=O…H-O, it was found that there was no COHP curve for CQDs/CuCoFe-300 (both bonding and antibonding components were zero), indicating that there was no chemical bonding at the interface, but rather an existence in the form of intermolecular interactions ( Fig. S10 ). In summary, after liquid-phase mixing, the C=O bond in CQDs tended to interact with the hydroxyl groups on the CuCoFe-300 surface in the form of C=O….…H-O hydrogen bonding due to electrostatic attraction (Fig. 2 g). Based on the electron gain or loss that might result from the shift of bonds in XPS, we speculated that the electronic properties of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 would change after the loading of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of interfacial electronic channel on CQDs/CuCoFe-300
ce:section.ce:para:
The electronic interactions and charge transfer modes at the interface of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 were investigated using DFT calculations and electrochemical methods. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the electronic localization function (ELF) of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 indicated that electrons were primarily distributed around the C=O bonds in the CQDs and the hydroxyl groups and OV on CuCoFe-300, showing a clear electron partitioning between the CQDs and surface hydroxyl groups. Specifically, a strong attractive among the C, O, and H atoms facilitated the formation of the C=O…H-O structure, which might provide pathway for electron transport. To further understand this mechanism, charge transfer pathways at the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 interface was explored by charge differential density (CDD) and potential energy distribution. Clearly, within the C=O bond of CQDs, electrons were primarily concentrated on the O atom, with alternating regions of electron depletion and accumulation observed (yellow indicating electron accumulation and blue indicating electron depletion) (Fig. 3 b). The O atom in C=O lost electrons, which were transferred to the hydroxyl groups on the CuCoFe-300 surface along the hydrogen bond. Interestingly, CDD showed that a small portion of the electrons transferred to the CuCoFe-300 surface accumulated near the hydroxyl groups, while the majority migrated to the area surrounding the OV, reflecting the alternating coverage of yellow regions. This behavior was attributed to the strong electron-absorbing characteristics of the OV [31]. These electron transport phenomena were entirely consistent with the shifted XPS peaks observed, confirming the effectiveness of the charge transfer. Furthermore, changes in potential energy further supported the above conclusions (Fig. 3 c). In CQDs/CuCoFe-300, the potential energy of CuCoFe-300 was lower than that of CQDs, indicating that electrons were more tended to transfer from CQDs to CuCoFe-300 via the hydrogen bond structure formed by interfacial interactions. To quantify the electronic transport properties of CQDs/CuCoFe-300, an evaluation was conducted using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The results revealed ( Fig. S11 ) that due to the enhancement of interfacial electron circulation capacity, the electron transfer rate of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 was 5.87 times that of CuCoFe-300, significantly accelerating the electron transfer process.

The electron distribution near the Fermi level provided insights into the electronic properties of CuCoFe-300 before and after loading with CQDs. The calculated PDOS of O atoms indicated that the O 2p states of both CuCoFe-300 and CQDs/CuCoFe-300 were located in the electron occupied area and unoccupied area (Fig. 3 d). Notably, the E − Ef of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 exhibited a blue shift, suggesting a possible downward shift in the energy level of the p-band center. As expected, the construction of interfacial hydrogen bonds significantly lowered the energy level of the p-band center of O atoms, indicating that more electrons were filled into the orbitals of O in CuCoFe-300 via hydrogen bonds, consistent with the results of Mulliken charge transfer test (Fig. 3 e). Furthermore, an analysis of the interaction between CQDs and different hydroxyl sites showed that CQDs preferentially bonded to hydroxyl groups closest to OV, specifically O3, with charge values of −0.73 to −0.78. More importantly, as the electron transfer rate increased, the charge density on the OV site rose from 1.017 to 1.124 e, a much larger increase compared to O3. This fully confirmed that electrons supplied by CQDs mainly gather around OV after passing through the hydrogen bond channel, reducing the Mulliken charge of the surrounding metal atoms while only a small amount of charge accumulated on the hydroxyl groups. These computational results were highly consistent with the XPS measurements. Overall, faster electron transfer efficiency and higher local charge density were more favorable for O2 activation, facilitating ROS formation, which was essential for the efficient degradation of organic pollutants.

The catalytic performance of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 was investigated by catalytic wet oxidation degradation of tetracycline (TC) at room temperature and pressure. As shown in Fig. 4
a, CQDs were ineffective in degrading TC. In contrast, CQDs/CuCoFe-300 effectively activated molecular oxygen, achieving a degradation efficiency of 90.0 % within 120 min, significantly surpassing the 62.1 % efficiency observed with CuCoFe-300. Furthermore, the kinetic analysis indicated that the degradation processes of all three materials adhered to the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics model (Fig. 4 b). Compared to CuCoFe-300, the reaction rate for TC degradation in CQDs/CuCoFe-300 was accelerated by a factor of 2.2. The mineralization rate serves as a critical indicator for assessing the depth of organic pollutant degradation by the catalyst. The mineralization rate of TC on the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst reached 58.7 % within 120 min, reflecting a 20.2 % increase compared to the CuCoFe-300 catalyst (38.5 %) (Fig. 4 c). Thus, the catalytic activity of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 was significantly enhanced by the construction of interfacial hydrogen bond electron channels.

Considering that the degradation activity of catalysts was significantly influenced by the environmental factors, the effects of pH and co-existing anions were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 d, CQDs/CuCoFe-300 demonstrated excellent suitability for TC degradation across a broad pH range (4–11), with the degradation rates exceeding 83 % under mild conditions. Interestingly, the degradation performance of TC slightly decreased with increasing pH, potentially involving variations in O2 reduction across different media. The conversion of O2 to ROS (e.g., •O2 −, 1O2, etc.) exhibited a higher redox potential under acidic conditions, leading to greater ROS production compared to in alkaline condition [32]. In catalytic oxidation reaction processes, anions (NO3 –, Cl-, SO4 2-, etc.) often react with •OH to form active species with lower redox potential, adversely affecting the catalytic process [33,34]. As a proof of concept, the effects of various coexisting anions on TC degradation efficiency using the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst were investigated (Fig. 4 e). Encouragingly, the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst maintained excellent degradation efficiency for TC (∼78 %) even at NO3 –, Cl-, SO4 2- concentrations of 5 g/L. This result demonstrated that TC degradation did not depend on the •OH oxidation. Furthermore, the experimental results indicated that a range of benzene derivatives (e.g., phenol and benzotriazole) and other antibiotics (e.g., oxytetracycline, ciprofloxacin, etc.) were effectively degraded by the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst, not just TC (Fig. 4 f). In conclusion, the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 catalyst serves as a novel environmental functional material characterized by resistance to environmental disturbances and excellent adaptability, showcasing immense superiority and potential in treating refractory organic wastewater. We conducted tests using primary effluent from a pharmaceutical factory (containing various antibiotics, chemical oxygen demand (COD) of approximately 1217 mg/L, and sulfate concentration of 3.8 g/L) to explore the practical application potential of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 material. The results indicated that the combination of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 with air (air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300) achieved a treatment efficiency of 61.9 % within 24 h, demonstrating its promising practicality ( Fig. S12 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of dominant ROS and its evolutionary process
ce:section.ce:para:
The dominant active species and ROS conversion process in the catalytic reaction were analyzed by chemical competition kinetics and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 5
a, tert-butanol (TBA) as a quencher of •OH [35], slightly inhibited the degradation of TC, proving that •OH was not the primary ROS in the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system. Compared with 90.0 % of TC degradation efficiency within 120 min without any scavenger, the addition of L-histidine (a 1O2 scavenger) greatly reduced TC degradation to only 21.3 % [36], demonstrating that 1O2 production was the primary driver of TC degradation. Furthermore, the addition of varying concentrations of L-histidine were employed to validate the selection of quencher concentration. Fig. S13 indicated that 20 mM of L-histidine was sufficient to completely inhibit 1O2 generation in the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system. The addition of Cr2O7 2-, an electronic scavenger [37], severely inhibited TC degradation. Notably, the inhibition effect was consistent with that of L-histidine, suggesting that the conversion of molecular oxygen to ROS occurred via an electron-mediated process. The electron quenching directly impeded the pathway for ROS formation (equation (1)-(2)), significantly impacting the progress of catalytic reaction. The degradation efficiency of TC was similarly affected upon the addition of p-benzoquinone (pBQ, •O2 − scavenger). Since •O2 − was reported to be a critical intermediate in the catalytic oxidation process induced by OVs in an air environment (containing O2), and O2 could be selectively adsorbed and activated at the bridge-bonded OVs [38]. The quenching of •O2 − profoundly affected the electron transfer process and further conversion of 1O2 (equation (2)), thereby influencing TC degradation performance. The quenching experiment results in the air/CuCoFe-300 system were largely consistent with those in the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system (Fig. 5 b).

Given that 1O2 was identified as the primary factor driving TC degradation, the amount of 1O2 generated in CQDs/CuCoFe-300 and CuCoFe-300 system was quantitatively analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5 c, a total of 8.69 × 10-4 μmol/L 1O2 was produced in the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system within 120 min, attributed to the construction of an interfacial hydrogen bond electron channels, and approximately 3.42 times the concentration of 1O2 in air/CuCoFe-300 system (2.54 × 10-4 μmol/L).

To trace the evolution of O2 from OVs on CQDs/CuCoFe-300, EPR measurements were conducted to further elucidate the ROS conversion process. As shown in Fig. 5 d-e, when 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 4-amino-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were introduced into the reaction system, DMPO-O2 - adduct and TEMP-1O2 adduct signals were observed, confirming the generation of •O2 − and 1O2. Moreover, compared to the air/CuCoFe-300 system, the DMPO-O2 - and TEMP-1O2 adduct signals were significantly enhanced in the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system, suggesting that considering the influences of interfacial hydrogen bond electron channels, the CQD/CuCoFe-300 catalyst could more effectively activate O2 to generate substantial amounts of •O2 − and 1O2, thereby facilitating TC degradation. These findings were consistent with the quantitative experiment and degradation phenomenon. Additionally, as depicted in Fig. 5 f, the signal of the TEMP-1O2 adduct in the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system decreased sharply upon the addition of pBQ, providing strong evidence for the involvement of •O2 − in the transition to 1O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synergistic mechanism between interfacial electronic channel and OVs
ce:section.ce:para:
The interface hydrogen bond electron channels could accelerate the charge transfer ability at the surface/interface and enhanced the charge density of OVs, thereby contributing to the generate of ROS and improving the catalytic efficiency. Consequently, the electron behavior at the catalyst interface warrants further investigated. The adsorption and activation process of O2 molecule on CuCoFe-300 and CQDs/CuCoFe-300 were simulated using DFT. As illustrated in Fig. 6
a, the adsorption energy (Eads) for O2 molecule increased from −0.27 eV on CuCoFe-300 to −0.34 eV on CQDs/CuCoFe-300, resulting in more Bader effective charges being accepted by O2 from CQDs/CuCoFe-300 (−0.48 e) compared to CuCoFe-300 (−0.31 e), which enhancement undoubtedly improved the electron utilization efficiency of OVs. Notably, the O-O bond length of oxygen molecule adsorbed on OV of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 was stretched from 1.209 Å to 1.29 Å, which was significantly longer than that of the O-O bond in the CuCoFe-300 model (1.27 Å). This elongation facilitated the breaking of the O-O bond and enhanced the activation of O2 molecules. These results clearly confirmed that CQDs/CuCoFe-300, with interface hydrogen bond electron channels, promoted the charge transfer processes and effectively enhanced electron utilization, thereby accelerating O2 activation and producing abundant ROS. This phenomenon accounted for the superior degradation performance of the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system compared to that of the air/CuCoFe-300 system.

Based on the above experimental results and DFT calculation analysis, the potential mechanism for TC degradation in air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system under room conditions was proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 6 b. During the liquid-phase mixing and ultrasonic treatment, interactions between CuCoFe-300 and CQDs were generated through electrostatic adsorption. Specifically, the C=O bonds on CQDs interacted with hydroxyl groups on CuCoFe-300 via C=O…H-O hydrogen bonds, forming interfacial hydrogen bond electron channels. As a strong electron acceptor, OVs attracts the delocalized π electrons of the C=O bond on the CQD surface by coulombic attraction [39]. The C=O…H-O hydrogen bonds not only provides a physical channel for electron transfer but also enhances the electron coupling efficiency through the hydrogen bond network, enabling the directional migration of electrons in CQD to the OVs site, forming a localized charge enrichment region. With the involvement of O2 molecules, O2 adsorbs at the OVs defect site in an end-on coordination mode, causing the O-O bond length to stretch from 1.209 Å to 1.27 Å, and the adsorption energy changes from −0.27 eV to −0.34 eV. During this process, the interfacial hydrogen bond electron channel synergizes with OVs, mainly manifested by a significant increase in electron transfer rate, local charge density, and OVs electron utilization efficiency. This activation process essentially involves the injection of OVs-enriched electrons into the antibonding π* orbital of O2, converting O2 from the triplet ground state to •O2 − and further promoting the generation of the singlet excited state. It is noteworthy that the cooperative valence changes of metal ions around OVs further stabilize the peroxy intermediate through the ligand field effect. In summary, the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 material with the interfacial hydrogen bond electron channels could more rapidly initiate catalytic reactions under mild conditions. Compared to the original CuCoFe-300 material, the electron transfer rate of CQDs/CuCoFe-300 increased by 5.87 times, and the generation of 1O2 increased by 3.42 times, resulting in an enhanced TC degradation performance of 90.0 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application potential of air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system
ce:section.ce:para: The application potential of the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system were evaluated through cycling performance of catalyst and energy consumption of the reaction system. As shown in Fig. S14 , the cyclic performance of the CQD/CuCoFe-300 material significantly outperforms that of CuCoFe-300, maintaining a degradation efficiency of over 64.7 % after the fifth cycle, compared to the initial performance. The formation of interfacial hydrogen bond electron channels, along with enhanced electron aggregation, improved the electron utilization efficiency of OVs in CQDs/CuCoFe-300, thereby reducing the likelihood of OV being deactivated by O2 filling. In contrast, the lower electron transfer performance of CuCoFe-300 made it its deactivation more pronounced. Furthermore, the energy consumption cost was an important indicator for practical applications. The energy consumption of the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system was assessed by electric energy per order of pollutant removal (EE/O), with the calculated results presented in Table 1 (the calculation process was detailed in Text S3). Based on the reaction conditions at room temperature and atmospheric pressure, using air as the oxidant, the total EE/O value of the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system was found to be 0.004 kWh·L-1, significantly outperforming most advanced oxidation technologies reported, demonstrating a notable energy consumption advantage. High-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) is considered an effective tool for elucidating the catalytic degradation pathway of TC [40]. Eight intermediates, including TC, were detected in the sample analysis ( Fig. S15 and S16 ). The electrophilic reagent 1O2 tends to attack the carbonyl (C=O) in the TC molecule, facilitating the formation of intermediate P1, which further drives the generation of intermediates such as P2, P3, and P7 by altering the overall electron cloud distribution of the molecule and reducing steric hindrance effects. Meanwhile, 1O2 preferentially attacks the amino group (–NH2) with less steric hindrance to form intermediate P4. After P4 undergoes deamination, the enriched 1O2 in the system further attacks the carbonyl, generating smaller components P5 and P6. As the reaction continues, TC is ultimately mineralized into CO2 and H2O. The ecological risk of the above intermediates was evaluated by measuring the acute toxicity of fish and algae ( Fig. S17 ), and the results showed that all intermediate products were essentially non-toxic within the 96h exposure period. These findings indicated that high-performance catalysts could not only effectively degrade antibiotics but also reduced operational costs. Consequently, the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system, which did not require the addition of expensive oxidants or extra energy inputs, showed promising application prospects for the remediation of antibiotic-contaminated water environments.

In summary, this study developed an efficient interfacial engineering strategy by constructing C=O…H-O hydrogen bond electronic channels between CQDs and the OV-rich CuCoFe-300. The interfacial hydrogen bonds serve a dual function: on one hand, they serve as channels for directional electron transfer from CQDs to CuCoFe-300, and on the other hand, they enhance electron coupling efficiency through the polarization effect of the hydrogen bond network, increasing the charge transfer rate by 5.87 times compared to the original CuCoFe-300, and driving a 3.42-fold increase in 1O2 generation in the CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system. The synergistic effect of interfacial hydrogen bonds and OVs not only enhances the local charge density at OVs but also stabilizes the peroxide intermediates through metal ion valence coordination. More importantly, the air/CQDs/CuCoFe-300 system achieved 90 % TC degradation efficiency under mild and high-salt conditions with ultra-low energy input, significantly outperforming traditional advanced oxidation processes in environmental adaptability. This work reveals the application potential of the interfacial hydrogen bond-mediated electron transfer mechanism in low-energy catalytic systems, providing a universal strategy for optimizing energy-saving wastewater treatment technologies with defect-rich metal oxide catalysts.
